notes: searches: ~14 web + ~10 local greps, clusters: 11. Doctrinally-central chapter (Sabbath/Sunday origins, papal-authority development). Bulk of needs_verification claims are KJV/ASV biblical quotations resolved via common-knowledge fast-path (verbatim, correct attribution -> supported). Real research concentrated on: Sunday-law origins (Ayer primary + Bacchiocchi academic + Andrews period), Catholic Sabbath-change quotes (Segur/Tuberville/catechism primaries), 538-1798 historicist scheme (anachronistic), Sabbath-at-creation (disputed vs critical OT scholarship), Millerite Sabbath origin, US founding, and all footnote-only quotes identified (733=Andrews, 740=Townsend, 741=Dublin Nation, 754=Elliott, 755/756=Waffle, 757=Tuberville, 758=Segur). Control sample of 3 needs_verification:false claims processed: GC-25-065, GC-25-089, GC-25-136.

GC-25-001.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful KJV rendering of Revelation 11:19; attribution to the Book of Revelation correct.

GC-25-002.rationale: [common_knowledge] 'Example and shadow of heavenly things' is Hebrews 8:5; the once-a-year opening of the most holy place on the Day of Atonement is Leviticus 16 / Hebrews 9:7. Attribution correct.

GC-25-003.rationale: Theological claim: prophetic identification of Revelation's heavenly-temple opening with Christ's 1844 sanctuary ministry (investigative judgment). Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-004.rationale: Theological/supernatural claim about believers beholding the ark of the testament by faith. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-005.rationale: [common_knowledge] The biblical account (Exodus 25:16; Deuteronomy 10:5; 1 Kings 8:9; Hebrews 9:4) states the ark held the two tables of stone inscribed with the Decalogue. Faithful report.

GC-25-006.rationale: Theological claim (God spoke and wrote the law with His own finger at Sinai). Rests on Exodus 20/31 narrative as divine act; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-007.rationale: Theological claim: a heavenly original law of which the Decalogue is a transcript. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-008.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful KJV rendering of Matthew 5:18; attribution to Jesus in the Gospel correct.

GC-25-009.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Psalm 119:89; attribution to the psalmist correct.

GC-25-010.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Psalm 111:7-8; attribution to Psalms correct.

GC-25-011.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful KJV rendering of the fourth commandment, Exodus 20:8-11; attribution correct.

GC-25-012.rationale: Theological/supernatural claim about the Spirit of God impressing hearts. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-013.rationale: Historically documented: post-1844 Millerite Adventists (Washington, NH group; T.M. Preble, Joseph Bates) became convicted of seventh-day Sabbath obligation and connected it to the fourth commandment. Millerite-cluster escalation; documented in scholarly Adventist historiography (G. Land, AUSS 1994, held locally) and standard reference works. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: History of the Seventh-day Adventist Church
ESDA: Bates, Joseph (1792-1872) — Adventist Encyclopedia

GC-25-014.rationale: Documented: through Rachel Oakes (Seventh Day Baptist), Frederick Wheeler, and T.M. Preble's 1845 tract, this group examined the basis for first-day vs seventh-day observance (1844-1846). source_quality: academic-partial (Adventist historiography, Land AUSS 1994 locally).

sources.title: History of the Seventh-day Adventist Church
ESDA: Bates, Joseph — Adventist Encyclopedia

GC-25-015.rationale: The group's conclusion that Scripture gives no command abolishing the fourth commandment or changing the Sabbath aligns with the mainstream scholarly finding of NT silence on any Sabbath-to-Sunday change (Bacchiocchi). Reports their reasoning faithfully. source_quality: academic-full.

sources.title: S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (Pontifical Gregorian Univ. Press, 1977), ch. 6

GC-25-016.rationale: Documented: this group began seventh-day Sabbath observance in 1844-1846. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: History of the Seventh-day Adventist Church

GC-25-017.rationale: Theological argument that accepting the heavenly-sanctuary doctrine entails Sabbath obligation. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-018.rationale: Interpretive/theological claim about the hidden cause of opposition to the sanctuary exposition. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-019.rationale: Theological metaphor (opening/shutting doors of divine ministration). Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-020.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Revelation 3:7; attribution to Christ in Revelation correct.

GC-25-021.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Revelation 3:8; attribution correct.

GC-25-022.rationale: Theological claim identifying the believers' doctrines with the content of Revelation 14. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-024.rationale: [common_knowledge] 'The hour of His judgment is come' is Revelation 14:7; attribution correct.

GC-25-025.rationale: Theological claim about the duration of Christ's intercession. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-034.rationale: Theological interpretation of the first angel's message requiring law-obedience. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-037.rationale: [common_knowledge] 'This is the love of God, that we keep His commandments' is 1 John 5:3; attribution correct.

GC-25-050.rationale: Footnote-733 quote identified and confirmed verbatim in J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873), ch. 27, held locally (lines ~23637-23646): 'The Sabbath therefore lies at the very foundation of divine worship ... the distinction between the Creator and his creatures.' Accurate quotation. source_quality: academic-partial (period Sabbatarian monograph, primary to the citation).

sources.title: J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873), ch. 27 — local file, verbatim match

GC-25-051.rationale: Genuine historiographical disagreement. Conservative reading takes Genesis 2:2-3 as instituting the Sabbath as a creation ordinance in Eden; critical OT scholarship (documentary hypothesis) assigns Genesis 1:1-2:3 to the Priestly source (6th c. BC) and dates the weekly Sabbath as an Israelite (Mosaic/exilic) institution, treating the creation-rest framing as etiological rather than evidence of Edenic observance. Doctrinally-central Sabbath-origin cluster. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: 'Tracing the Origin of the Sabbatical Calendar in the Priestly Narrative' (Genesis 1 to Joshua 5)
'The Sabbath: mark of distinction', Themelios (The Gospel Coalition)

GC-25-052.rationale: Theological claim about the Sabbath's enduring role as sign of creatorship. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-053.rationale: Counterfactual/interpretive theological claim (universal Sabbath-keeping would have prevented idolatry, atheism, infidelity). Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-054.rationale: Theological claim (Sabbath as sign of loyalty). Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-055.rationale: Interpretive/theological inference about the Revelation 14 message. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-056.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Revelation 14:9-10; attribution correct.

GC-25-057.rationale: [common_knowledge] Revelation 12 does depict a dragon poised to devour the woman's child at its birth (12:4); accurate textual report of the Book of Revelation.

GC-25-058.rationale: Theological identification (dragon = Satan, who moved Herod). The dragon-Satan identification is prophetic exegesis; the underlying Herod historical claim is logged separately at GC-25-059.

GC-25-059.rationale: The claim faithfully reports the Gospel of Matthew's infancy narrative (Matthew 2:13-18), in which Herod the Great seeks to kill the infant Jesus (Massacre of the Innocents). Textual attribution is accurate. Modern historians debate the event's historicity (uncorroborated outside Matthew, absent from Josephus), but the claim as framed reports what Matthew states, which is correct.

sources.title: Massacre of the Innocents — historicity discussion

GC-25-060.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Roman Empire's prevailing state religion in the first Christian centuries was pagan, and Rome periodically persecuted Christians (Nero, Decius, Diocletian). Well-established general history; the 'chief agent of Satan' framing is theological overlay, not the checkable core.

GC-25-061.rationale: Theological/interpretive identification of the Revelation 12 dragon with Satan (primary) and pagan Rome (secondary). Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-063.rationale: Historiographically accurate: from the Reformation through the 19th century most Protestant interpreters (the historicist school) identified the leopard-like beast of Revelation 13 / little horn of Daniel 7 with the papacy. Well documented in the history of biblical interpretation. The claim reports the prevalence of the view, not its validity.

sources.title: Historicism (Christian eschatology) — Protestant identification of the papacy
Antichrist — Protestant Reformation identification with the papacy

GC-25-064.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Revelation 13:5-7; attribution correct.

GC-25-065.rationale: [control sample; extractor flagged needs_verification:false] Checkable textual observation: the Revelation 13 beast description (blasphemy, war on the saints, a defined time-period of dominion) closely parallels the Daniel 7 'little horn' (Dan 7:8,20-25). The parallel is a real feature of the texts; the further identification with the papacy is separate exegesis. No smuggled false fact.

GC-25-069.rationale: Theological interpretation equating Revelation's 42 months with Daniel 7's 'time, times and dividing of time' = 1260 prophetic days/years of papal oppression. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-070.rationale: The 538-1798 span for the 1260-year period of papal supremacy is a historicist-school construction, not a datum of mainstream historiography. The end anchor (1798, capture of Pius VI) is a real event, but 538 is chosen to fit the 1260-year scheme (linked to Justinian/Belisarius and the Ostrogothic withdrawal); historians and even Adventist critics note it marked a nadir, not the zenith, of papal power and is not an independently attested start of 'papal supremacy.' Consistent with the interpretive scheme in White's sources (Andrews, Uriah Smith) and with the anachronistic/disputed pattern recorded for chapters 15/18/22.
GC-25-070.modern_consensus: No mainstream historian dates a 1260-year 'papal supremacy' from A.D. 538; the date is a historicist calculation working backward from 1798.
GC-25-070.period_historiography: The 538-1798 = 1260-year scheme was standard in 19th-century Adventist/Millerite historicism (Andrews, Uriah Smith) built on earlier Protestant year-day interpretation.

sources.title: 1798: Pope captured and taken from Rome; historicist 538/1260 framework
'Case by Casebolt: The 1260 Year Prophecy Problem', Spectrum

GC-25-071.rationale: Historically accurate core: in February 1798 General Berthier's French army entered Rome, proclaimed a republic, and took Pope Pius VI prisoner (he died in French captivity at Valence in 1799). The 'deadly wound'/prophecy-fulfillment framing is theological overlay; the event itself is well documented.

sources.title: Pope Pius VI — capture by French forces, 1798 (Wikipedia)
History Skills — Berthier and the Pope, 1798

GC-25-073.rationale: [common_knowledge] Daniel 7:2-3; faithful rendering, attribution to Daniel correct.

GC-25-075.rationale: Interpretive symbol-scheme (winds = strife; four winds on the sea = conquest/revolution). Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-076.rationale: Interpretive inference deriving a geographic location (Western Hemisphere) from the prophetic symbol. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-077.rationale: In 1798 the United States was a young nation rising in strength and drawing international attention (the Quasi-War with France and the XYZ Affair placed it prominently before the world that very year). The identification of it as the prophesied nation is separate interpretation; the historical characterization of a rising New World power in 1798 is defensible.

sources.title: Quasi-War / XYZ Affair, 1798 — U.S. Dept. of State, Office of the Historian

GC-25-078.rationale: Interpretive claim that the prophecy points exclusively to the United States. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-079.rationale: Interpretive claim that orators/historians unconsciously echoed the prophecy's language. Rhetorical/interpretive; not empirically checkable as stated.

GC-25-080.rationale: The Greek verb behind 'coming up' in Revelation 13:11 is anabaino, which lexica (Liddell-Scott) attest is used of plants shooting up/springing up (cf. Matthew 13:7, thorns that 'grew up' = anabaino). The claim that translators note it can signify to grow/spring up as a plant has lexical support.

sources.title: anabaino (G305) — lexical usage incl. plants springing up

GC-25-081.rationale: Footnote-740 'prominent writer' identified as George Alfred Townsend, The New World Compared with the Old (1869), a real work by a real author. Exact wording ('mystery of her coming forth from vacancy'; 'Like a silent seed we grew into empire') is the standard attribution reproduced in the GC appendix; snippet search confirmed author/title but did not surface the verbatim lines, hence medium confidence.

sources.title: G.A. Townsend, The New World Compared with the Old (1869) — Internet Archive

GC-25-082.rationale: Footnote-741 'European journal in 1850' is identified in the GC appendix as The Dublin Nation (1850). The quotation is the standard attribution; unable to surface the verbatim 1850 issue in snippet search, hence medium confidence. No evidence of misquotation.

sources.title: GC 1911 Appendix — 'The United States in Prophecy' source notes

GC-25-083.rationale: Edward Everett delivered an oration at Plymouth on 22 December 1824 on the Pilgrim founders; the quoted phrases ('the little church of Leyden', 'peaceful conquest ... they have borne the banners of the cross') appear in the published oration (Making of America / Univ. of Michigan). Accurate attribution and quotation.

sources.title: Edward Everett, oration at Plymouth (1824), Making of America — Univ. of Michigan Library

GC-25-084.rationale: Interpretive claim reading the lamb-like horns as symbolizing US youth/innocence/gentleness in 1798. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-085.rationale: [common_knowledge] Among early Christian settlers in America were groups fleeing royal oppression and religious intolerance who sought to found governments on civil and religious liberty (Pilgrims/Puritans, Roger Williams in Rhode Island, William Penn's Pennsylvania, Lord Baltimore's Maryland). Standard general history.

GC-25-086.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim from the Declaration of Independence (1776): 'all men are created equal ... life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness.' Attribution correct.

GC-25-087.rationale: [common_knowledge] The U.S. Constitution establishes representative self-government (Article I: laws made by elected representatives). Accurate characterization.

GC-25-088.rationale: [common_knowledge] The First Amendment (1791) guarantees free exercise of religion; the founding framework granted religious liberty. Accurate.

GC-25-089.rationale: [control sample; extractor flagged needs_verification:false] Largely interpretive, but carries a defensible factual core: the early United States was overwhelmingly Protestant in religious composition and republican in government, and these were widely regarded (then and by many historians since) as formative national principles. Characterization is defensible; not a smuggled false fact. Causal claim about their being 'the secret of' national power is opinion (see GC-25-090).

GC-25-090.rationale: Interpretive/causal opinion (republicanism + Protestantism = the cause of US power and prosperity). A value judgment, not empirically decidable.

GC-25-091.rationale: [common_knowledge] Millions of immigrants, many fleeing oppression, emigrated to the United States through the 19th century, and the US rose to become one of the world's most powerful nations. Standard demographic/historical fact.

GC-25-092.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Revelation 13:11-14; attribution correct.

GC-25-093.rationale: Theological prediction of a future development of intolerance/persecution in the US. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-094.rationale: Theological prediction that US authority will enforce homage to the papacy. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-095.rationale: [common_knowledge] First clause is the First Amendment establishment/free-exercise clause; second is Article VI, clause 3 ('no religious Test shall ever be required as a Qualification to any Office or public Trust under the United States'). Both verbatim; attribution correct.

GC-25-096.rationale: [common_knowledge] The framers deliberately separated church and state (establishment clause, no-religious-test clause) to prevent state-enforced religion; the historical intent to guard against church use of secular coercion is well documented. The 'inevitable result' phrasing is interpretive.

GC-25-097.rationale: Interpretive claim reading the two-horned beast as evidence the US is the prophesied nation. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-098.rationale: Interpretive instruction to study the papacy to understand the 'image.' Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-099.rationale: Doctrinally-central (papal-authority development). The checkable core — that the post-Constantinian church allied with imperial power and, absorbing surrounding cultural forms, increasingly used state support to control belief — is broadly supported by mainstream church history (Constantine 313, Theodosius' 380 Edict of Thessalonica making Nicene Christianity the state religion). The polemical framing ('lost the Spirit and power of God', 'heathen rites') is Protestant historiography, not the checkable component. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (Pontifical Gregorian Univ. Press, 1977), ch. 6 — church-state relations and Roman influence
Edict of Thessalonica (380) — state establishment of Nicene Christianity

GC-25-100.rationale: The church's alliance with imperial power and use of the state to punish heresy is historically attested (e.g., the execution of Priscillian in 385, the first Christian put to death by Christian authority for heresy; later imperial anti-heresy legislation). 'The papacy' as the singular product is a compressed, teleological framing typical of Protestant historiography, but the church-state-coercion core is supported. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: Priscillian — first execution for heresy under Christian authority (385)
S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (1977), ch. 6

GC-25-101.rationale: Theological/conditional claim defining what would constitute the US forming an 'image of the beast.' Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-102.rationale: Sweeping interpretive generalization ('whenever the church has obtained secular power, she has punished dissent'). A rhetorical universal, not a discrete checkable fact.

GC-25-103.rationale: Interpretive generalization about Protestant churches allied with worldly powers. Not a discrete checkable fact as stated.

GC-25-104.rationale: The Church of England conducted long-continued persecution of dissenters (Puritans, Separatists, Nonconformists, Quakers) via the Act of Uniformity, the Clarendon Code (Conventicle Act, Five Mile Act), and earlier Elizabethan/Jacobean measures. Well documented.

sources.title: Great Ejection (1662) and the Clarendon Code — persecution of Nonconformists
Act of Uniformity 1662

GC-25-105.rationale: Documented: the Great Ejection of 1662 forced roughly 2,000-2,500 Nonconformist ministers from their livings, and under the Clarendon Code many pastors and laypeople suffered fines, imprisonment, and worse across the 16th-17th centuries. 'Thousands ... torture and martyrdom' is broadly accurate for the era (including earlier Marian and Elizabethan persecutions), with the ejection figures well attested.

sources.title: Great Ejection — c. 2,000-2,500 ministers ejected 1662; subsequent persecution
The Great Ejection — Nonconformists and 1662, Evangelical Times

GC-25-106.rationale: [common_knowledge] 2 Thessalonians 2:3 ('a falling away ... that man of sin be revealed'); attribution to Paul correct.

GC-25-107.rationale: The checkable core — that early-church accommodation and pursuit of civil support preceded and enabled the medieval papal power — aligns with mainstream church history on the Constantinian church-state settlement. 'Apostasy' is a theological label for the same development. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: Edict of Thessalonica (380) and Constantinian church-state development
S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (1977), ch. 6

GC-25-108.rationale: Theological prediction that future apostasy will prepare the 'image to the beast.' Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-109.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of 2 Timothy 3:1-5; attribution to Paul correct.

GC-25-111.rationale: [common_knowledge] Composite of 2 Thessalonians 2:9-11; faithful rendering, attribution correct.

GC-25-112.rationale: Theological claim about end-time religious declension mirroring the early centuries. Prophetic interpretation; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-113.rationale: There was a real and growing 19th-century Protestant movement toward union on shared doctrine (the Evangelical Alliance, founded London 1846, an early ecumenical body organized around common evangelical points). Accurate.

sources.title: Evangelical Alliance (1846) — Britannica

GC-25-114.rationale: Interpretive inference that doctrinal union requires waiving disputed subjects. Opinion; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-115.rationale: Charles Beecher preached 'The Bible a Sufficient Creed' in 1846 (dedication of his church, Feb. 22, 1846; later published in Boston), an anti-creedal sermon warning that substituting creeds is 'a step in apostasy' and drawing the Rome parallel; the year, author, and thrust match. Verbatim confirmation of the full quoted passage ('another general council ... universal creed') not surfaced independently, hence medium confidence, but no sign of misquotation.

sources.title: 'The First Step in Apostasy: The Anti-Creedalism of Charles Beecher', Spectrum Magazine
'The Bible a Sufficient Creed' (Charles Beecher, 1846) — discussion, Fulcrum7

GC-25-116.rationale: Theological prediction that uniformity efforts will lead to force. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-117.rationale: Theological/conditional prediction about US churches forming an 'image of the Roman hierarchy.' Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-118.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Revelation 13:16-17; attribution correct.

GC-25-119.rationale: [common_knowledge] Revelation 14:9 (third angel's warning; repeat of GC-25-056); faithful rendering, attribution correct.

GC-25-120.rationale: Theological interpretation of the 'image to the beast' as future apostate Protestantism. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-121.rationale: [common_knowledge] Revelation 14:12 (repeat of GC-25-028); faithful rendering, attribution correct.

GC-25-122.rationale: Theological claim that law-keeping vs violation marks the two classes. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-123.rationale: [common_knowledge] Daniel 7:25 ('think to change the times and the law'; KJV: 'think to change times and laws'); faithful rendering, attribution to Daniel correct. The application to the papacy is separate interpretation.

GC-25-124.rationale: [common_knowledge] Paul styles the power 'man of sin ... who exalteth himself above ... God' in 2 Thessalonians 2:3-4; attribution correct. The papal identification is separate interpretation.

GC-25-125.rationale: Theological claim (papacy exalts itself by changing God's law; keeping the changed law honors that power). Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-126.rationale: Doctrinally-central. Two checkable pieces: (a) Catholic catechisms omit the images clause as a separate commandment — factually they follow the Augustinian enumeration that folds the images prohibition into the first commandment and splits coveting into the ninth/tenth; this is a numbering tradition (Augustine), not a papal deletion of the text from Scripture. (b) The seventh-to-first-day shift was a gradual development of the early centuries, not a decreed papal act. The polemical framing 'the papacy has attempted to change the law of God' matches White's sources (Andrews, Dowling) rather than modern historiography. source_quality: academic-full.
GC-25-126.modern_consensus: The Decalogue numbering is Augustinian (adopted by Catholics and Lutherans), not a papal suppression; the Sunday transition was a gradual early-church development, not a papal legislative change to the fourth commandment.
GC-25-126.period_historiography: 19th-century Sabbatarian works (J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath; Dowling, History of Romanism) presented the papacy as deliberately altering the Decalogue.

sources.title: S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (Pontifical Gregorian Univ. Press, 1977) — gradual development of Sunday, not a papal decree
J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873) — period source asserting papal change

GC-25-127.rationale: Accurate description of Catholic catechetical practice: the tradition following Augustine's enumeration treats the prohibition of images as included within the first commandment (against false worship), which is exactly the rationale Catholic apologists give. The claim reports their argument, which they do make.

sources.title: Ten Commandments — differing enumeration (Augustinian/Catholic vs others)
Catechism of the Catholic Church — treatment of the first commandment and images

GC-25-128.rationale: Theological claim that the Daniel 7:25 prophecy is 'exactly fulfilled' by the Sabbath change. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-129.rationale: Theological claim that only church authority is cited for Sunday, and that this sets the papacy above God. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-130.rationale: Theological prediction distinguishing worshipers of God vs the beast by Sabbath vs Sunday. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-131.rationale: Doctrinally-central Sunday-law-origins cluster. Academic scholarship (Bacchiocchi) credits the church of Rome with a leading role in the adoption of Sunday, so the claim that Rome championed Sunday has support. But 'popery first asserted its arrogant claims' on behalf of Sunday and that 'its first resort to the power of the state was to compel the observance of Sunday' conflates papal authority with Constantine's civil Sunday edict of A.D. 321 (a pagan/imperial law, printed in Ayer's Source Book) and the Council of Laodicea (c. 360) — neither an exercise of papal state power. The framing traces to J.N. Andrews' History of the Sabbath (footnote 751). source_quality: academic-full.
GC-25-131.modern_consensus: The first Sunday legislation was Constantine's civil edict of 321, not a papal act; the church of Rome influenced Sunday's rise, but 'papal' state enforcement of Sunday is a later, retrojected framing.
GC-25-131.period_historiography: J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873), presents Rome/'popery' as the first to demand Sunday observance and to invoke state power for it — the source behind this sentence (GC footnote 751).

sources.title: J.C. Ayer, A Source Book for Ancient Church History (1913) — Constantine's Sunday law of 321 ('venerable Day of the Sun'), local file
S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (Pontifical Gregorian Univ. Press, 1977), ch. 6 — role of the church of Rome
J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873) — period source, local file

GC-25-132.rationale: [common_knowledge] 'The Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath' — Mark 2:28 / Luke 6:5; attribution to Christ correct.

GC-25-133.rationale: [common_knowledge] Exodus 20:10 (repeat of the fourth-commandment text); faithful rendering, attribution correct.

GC-25-134.rationale: [common_knowledge] Isaiah 58:13 designates the Sabbath 'my holy day'; attribution to Isaiah correct.

GC-25-135.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Matthew 5:17-19 (Sermon on the Mount); attribution to Christ correct.

GC-25-136.rationale: [control sample; extractor flagged needs_verification:false] Theological/interpretive argument that Christ's words (Matthew 5:17-19, already verified) disprove the claim that He changed the Sabbath. No smuggled checkable fact beyond the scripture citation itself; verdict stands as unverifiable interpretation.

GC-25-137.rationale: It is indeed widely acknowledged, including by Protestant scholars and confirmed by mainstream academic study, that the New Testament contains no explicit command transferring the Sabbath from the seventh to the first day; the change is generally recognized as a post-apostolic development. source_quality: academic-full.

sources.title: S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (Pontifical Gregorian Univ. Press, 1977), ch. 6 — no NT authority for the change
George Elliott, The Abiding Sabbath (ATS prize essay) — acknowledges NT silence

GC-25-138.rationale: The two works are identified (GC 1911 Appendix): George Elliott, The Abiding Sabbath (an American Tract Society prize essay) and A.E. Waffle, The Lord's Day (American Sunday-school Union). Both concede the absence of explicit NT authority for the day-change, as quoted at GC-25-139/140/141. Attribution to ATS and ASU publications is correct.

sources.title: GC 1911 Appendix source notes — Elliott (ATS) and Waffle (ASU)

GC-25-139.rationale: Footnote-754 quote identified: George Elliott, The Abiding Sabbath, p. 184 ('the complete silence of the New Testament so far as any explicit command for the Sabbath [Sunday] ... are concerned'). Accurate quotation of a real ATS prize-essay.

sources.title: George Elliott, The Abiding Sabbath, p. 184 (via GC 1911 Appendix)
The Abiding Sabbath — George Elliott (Google Books record)

GC-25-140.rationale: Footnote-755 quote identified: A.E. Waffle, The Lord's Day, pp. 186-188 ('Up to the time of Christ's death, no change had been made in the day'). Accurate quotation of a real ASU work.

sources.title: A.E. Waffle, The Lord's Day, pp. 186-188 (via GC 1911 Appendix)

GC-25-141.rationale: Footnote-756 quote identified: A.E. Waffle, The Lord's Day, pp. 186-188 ('so far as the record shows, they [the apostles] did not ... give any explicit command enjoining the abandonment of the seventh-day Sabbath ...'). Accurate quotation.

GC-25-142.rationale: Doctrinally-central. Roman Catholic catechetical and apologetic literature does explicitly claim the Church transferred solemnity from Saturday to Sunday and cites Protestant Sunday-keeping as tacit recognition of that authority (Segur; Tuberville; the Catechism of Trent tradition). Academic scholarship (Bacchiocchi) confirms the church of Rome's leading role in the change historically. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: L.G. de Segur, Plain Talk about the Protestantism of To-day (1868), p. 213
S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (1977), ch. 6 — Rome's role in the change

GC-25-143.rationale: Doctrinally-central attributed quote. The passage ('During the old law, Saturday was the day sanctified; but the church, instructed by Jesus Christ ... has substituted Sunday for Saturday ...') is widely and consistently reproduced as from a 'Catholic Catechism of Christian Religion' and matches the authentic Catholic catechetical position (the Church claims authority to have transferred the day). The specific catechism is obscure and could not be located as a verbatim primary within budget, so medium confidence; the substance is a genuine, well-attested Catholic position, not a fabrication. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: L.G. de Segur, Plain Talk about the Protestantism of To-day — parallel Catholic authority claim
S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday (1977), ch. 6

GC-25-144.rationale: Footnote-757 quote identified and confirmed: Henry Tuberville, An Abridgment of the Christian Doctrine, p. 58 ('By the very act of changing the Sabbath into Sunday, which Protestants allow of ... because by keeping Sunday, they acknowledge the church's power to ordain feasts, and to command them under sin'). Accurate quotation of a real Catholic catechetical work.

sources.title: H. Tuberville, An Abridgment of the Christian Doctrine, p. 58 — quoted passage
Catholic Sabbath-change statements — Tuberville quotation

GC-25-145.rationale: Interpretive/theological claim that Sunday-keeping tacitly concedes Rome's supremacy. Opinion/prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-146.rationale: The formula 'The Bible, and the Bible only, is the religion of Protestants' is the famous epigram of William Chillingworth, The Religion of Protestants a Safe Way to Salvation (1637): 'The BIBLE, I say, the Bible only, is the religion of Protestants.' Genuine and correctly used as the sola-scriptura principle separating Protestants from Rome.

sources.title: William Chillingworth, The Religion of Protestants (1637) — Internet Archive
William Chillingworth — Wikipedia (the epigram)

GC-25-147.rationale: Interpretive claim about how Catholics perceive Protestant self-deception. Opinion; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-148.rationale: At the time of writing (late 1880s), a movement for civil enforcement of Sunday observance was indeed gaining ground in the US: the Blair Sunday-Rest Bill (introduced May 1888) and the National Reform Association / W.F. Crafts campaign, backed by petitions claiming millions of signatures. Accurate historical claim; the predicted outcome is separate theology.

sources.title: The Blair Sunday-Rest Bill (1888) and the national Sunday-law campaign — Liberty Magazine
'The Sunday Rest Bill and the Battle to Keep the Civil Sabbath' (Seton Hall Law scholarship)

GC-25-149.rationale: Footnote-758 quote identified: Mgr L.G. de Segur, Plain Talk about the Protestantism of To-day (1868), p. 213 ('The observance of Sunday by the Protestants is an homage they pay, in spite of themselves, to the authority of the [Catholic] church'). Accurate quotation of a real Catholic work.

sources.title: L.G. de Segur, Plain Talk about the Protestantism of To-day (1868), p. 213
Segur quotation reproduced in Catholic/Protestant Sunday-statement compilations

GC-25-150.rationale: Theological claim that knowing Sunday-keepers pay homage to the commanding power. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-151.rationale: Theological/conditional claim that enforcing Sunday would form an 'image to the beast' = enforcing beast-worship. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-152.rationale: [common_knowledge] Christians of past generations did observe Sunday sincerely believing it the Christian Sabbath / Lord's Day. Uncontroversial historical characterization of ordinary Christian belief.

GC-25-153.rationale: Theological claim about sincere Sunday-keepers' standing before God. Not empirically checkable.

GC-25-154.rationale: Theological prediction about a future enforced-Sunday scenario and its moral consequences. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-155.rationale: Theological claim that rejecting the Sabbath for Rome's Sunday = accepting the 'mark of the beast.' Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-156.rationale: [common_knowledge] Revelation 14:6 ('every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people'); faithful rendering, attribution correct.

GC-25-158.rationale: Theological claim about the warning being given before God's judgments. Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-159.rationale: Theological prediction (church-state union compelling the mark; God's people refusing). Prophetic exegesis; not empirically checkable.

GC-25-160.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Revelation 15:2-3; 'the prophet of Patmos' (John) is the conventional attribution for Revelation. Correct.
